translation: By the mount
commentary:
وَٱلطُّورِ

Allah ﷻ begins this powerful surah with a series of oaths, and the opening passages of At-Tur contain some of the most discussed verses in Quranic exegesis. There are numerous scholarly opinions about what each oath refers to, and what follows represents one coherent understanding among several valid interpretations.

The surah opens with "by the Tur" - الطُّور . This refers to the mountain where Allah ﷻ spoke directly with Prophet Musa (AS), though in Arabic, any great mountain can be called "tur." The presence of the definite article (al) suggests this is the specific mountain of revelation, making it a profound symbol of divine communication with humanity.

translation: And [by] a Book inscribed
commentary:
وَكِتَـٰبٍ مَّسْطُورٍ

"And by a book that has been written out" - كِتَابٍ مَسْطُورٍ . While this could refer to the Quran itself, which was revealed as divine words upon the heart of the Prophet ﷺ, keeping with the sequence of the previous oath points us toward the tablets given to Musa (AS). The word مَسْطُور  means laid out line by line, well-documented and preserved.

The people of the Book were people of reading and writing. They documented the Torah on scrolls upon scrolls, creating extensive written records of revelation.

translation: In parchment spread open
commentary:
فِى رَقٍّ مَّنشُورٍ

"In thin, spread-out parchment" - رَقٍّ مَنشُورٍ . The word رَقّ  comes from the root meaning soft and fine. This is why in modern Arabic, when you want to tell someone to go easy on another person or give them something to soften their heart, the word used is from this same root - it means to soften.

This ayah evokes the image of the scrolls of the scholars of Bani Israel, laid out one after another, containing their written works and documentation of divine guidance. The imagery is vivid: delicate parchment spread out for reading and study.

translation: And [by] the frequented House
commentary:
وَٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْمَعْمُورِ

"And by the house that is well-populated" - الْبَيْتِ الْمَعْمُورِ . The word مَعْمُور  refers to a place that is bustling with people, well-visited and thriving. In Arabic, when you say a city has many residents, you describe it as عامر  or معمور .

According to the hadith of Isra and Mi'raj, the Prophet ﷺ referred to Al-Bayt Al-Ma'mur as the Kaaba of the seventh heaven. We learn that every sky has its own Kaaba above the one we have on earth. Some scholars hold that this could also refer, by extension, to the entire world - this populated house that is our planet.

translation: And [by] the heaven raised high
commentary:
وَٱلسَّقْفِ ٱلْمَرْفُوعِ

"And by the elevated roof" - السَّقْفِ الْمَرْفُوعِ . This opinion that Ayah 4 refers to the world is strengthened by this ayah describing the high, elevated ceiling - the sky itself. If the previous ayah speaks of the populated house of this world, then this describes its magnificent roof raised high above us.

The sequence of oaths now becomes clear: on one hand, we have the great revelations to Musa (AS), and on the other, we have Makkah and by extension the entire inhabited world with its celestial ceiling.

translation: And [by] the sea filled [with fire]
commentary:
وَٱلْبَحْرِ ٱلْمَسْجُورِ

"And by the ocean that is set to boil" - الْبَحْرِ الْمَسْجُورِ . The word سَجَر  refers to intense boiling where water rises because of the heat, as if something from underneath pushed it up until it erupts. We encounter this same root at the end of Surah At-Takwir: وَإِذَا الْبِحَارُ سُجِّرَتْ - "And when the seas are set ablaze."

Here مَسْجُور  - in the passive form - indicates that the ocean has been made this way by Allah ﷻ, that it is meant to be raised and caused to boil in this manner.

translation: Indeed, the punishment of your Lord will occur
commentary:
إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ لَوَٰقِعٌ

After these powerful oaths establishing Allah's signs in creation and revelation, the conclusion arrives: "Indeed, the punishment of your Master is bound to happen" - إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ لَوَاقِعٌ. The word لَوَاقِعٌ  emphasizes absolute certainty - this punishment will occur without any doubt.

Notice how Allah ﷻ says "your Master" - رَبِّكَ  - creating a personal connection. This is your Master, the One who created you, sustained you, and now warns you with absolute certainty.

translation: Of it there is no preventer
commentary:
مَّا لَهُۥ مِن دَافِعٍ

"There is nothing that can push it away" - مَا لَهُ مِن دَافِعٍ . No one will be able to defend against it, cancel it, nullify it, or prevent it from occurring. The punishment that has been decreed will come, and no power in creation can stop it.

translation: On the Day the heaven will sway with circular motion
commentary:
يَوْمَ تَمُورُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ مَوْرًا

"The day when the sky will rattle relentlessly" - يَوْمَ تَمُورُ السَّمَاءُ مَوْرًا. The word مَوْر  describes intense shaking and rattling. It's used when a she-camel runs, swaying side to side in her motion. It also refers to movement that raises dust - when you drag your feet or something vibrates so quickly that it kicks up dust from the ground.

Allah ﷻ is describing the sky itself vibrating violently, shaking in a way that we cannot even comprehend.

translation: And the mountains will pass on, departing
commentary:
وَتَسِيرُ ٱلْجِبَالُ سَيْرًا

"And the mountains will sail away" - وَتَسِيرُ الْجِبَالُ سَيْرًا. The word سَيْر  in Arabic refers to easy, effortless, smooth movement - like sailing. The mountains, these massive, immovable structures, will simply glide away as if weightless. Later in the Quran, we find them described as كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنفُوشِ - like tufts of carded wool floating in the air, like cotton candy drifting on the wind.

translation: Then woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
فَوَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ

"Then terrible destruction on that Day will be for those who denied" - فَوَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ. The word وَيْل  expresses severe punishment and destruction. The مُكَذِّبِين  are those who continuously denied - denied the message, denied the Prophet ﷺ, denied Allah ﷻ, denied revelation, denied the consequences of their actions.

translation: Who are in [empty] discourse amusing themselves
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ

"Those who in pointless activities were playing around" - الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ. The word خَوْض  literally means treading water. When you walk on solid ground, your feet are stable and you're safe. But when you wade into a lake where you can't see the bottom clearly, you don't know what you're stepping on. It could be a predator, a sharp rock, or a sudden drop where you plunge completely under. The English expression "treading dangerous waters" captures this perfectly.

These people were engaged in risky business, playing with fire, treating serious matters as games - يَلْعَبُونَ  - constantly playing around with what should have been taken seriously.

translation: The Day they are thrust toward the fire of Hell with a [violent] thrust, [its angels will say]
commentary:
يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلَىٰ نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا

"The Day when they will be pushed toward the Fire of Hell, pushed forcefully" - يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلَى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا. The repetition of the word دَعّ  - being pushed over and over again - emphasizes the force and humiliation of being shoved toward the punishment they denied.

translation: This is the Fire which you used to deny
commentary:
هَـٰذِهِ ٱلنَّارُ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ

"This is the Fire that you used to deny" - هَذِهِ النَّارُ الَّتِي كُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ. They will be confronted with the reality they spent their lives rejecting. The Fire they mocked, the punishment they laughed at, the consequences they dismissed - now undeniably real before them.

translation: Then is this magic, or do you not see
commentary:
أَفَسِحْرٌ هَـٰذَآ أَمْ أَنتُمْ لَا تُبْصِرُونَ

"Is this magic, or are you still unable to see?" - أَفَسِحْرٌ هَذَا أَمْ أَنتُمْ لَا تُبْصِرُونَ. The bitter irony strikes: they used to call the Prophet's message "magic" - سِحْر . Now they stand before the reality, and the question is thrown back at them: Is this magic too? Or are you still blind, still refusing to see what's right in front of you?

translation: [Enter to] burn therein; then be patient or impatient - it is all the same for you. You are only being recompensed [for] what you used to do
commentary:
ٱصْلَوْهَا فَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ أَوْ لَا تَصْبِرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

"Enter it! Whether you endure patiently or not, it's all the same for you. You are only being repaid for what you used to do" - اصْلَوْهَا فَاصْبِرُوا أَوْ لَا تَصْبِرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ. Cry or don't cry. Scream or stay silent. It makes no difference now. The time for choices has passed. إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ - you are only receiving the consequences of what you yourselves did, what you continuously chose to do.

translation: Indeed, the righteous will be in gardens and pleasure
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَنَعِيمٍ

Now the scene shifts dramatically. "Indeed, those who were mindful of Allah are in gardens and blessings" - إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَنَعِيمٍ. The المُتَّقِين  - those who protected themselves through consciousness of Allah ﷻ, who were cautious and careful about their relationship with their Creator - they are in multiple gardens, in continuous, abundant blessings.

translation: Enjoying what their Lord has given them, and their Lord protected them from the punishment of Hellfire
commentary:
فَـٰكِهِينَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ وَوَقَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

"Joyous because of what their Master granted them, and their Master protected them from the punishment of the blazing flame" - فَاكِهِينَ بِمَا آتَاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ وَوَقَاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذَابَ الْجَحِيمِ. They are فَاكِهِين  - filled with joy and delight. Notice the gifts multiply: gardens, continuous blessings, joy from what Allah ﷻ gives them, and protection from the Fire.

The ayah speaks in third person - "they" are joyous, "their" Master granted them, "their" Master protected them. The listener might think, "Yes, those fortunate people, not me."

translation: [They will be told], "Eat and drink in satisfaction for what you used to do
commentary:
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

But suddenly, the address shifts: "Eat and drink carefree, because of what you used to do" - كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا هَنِيئًا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ. This is second person - YOU eat and drink! Allah ﷻ doesn't maintain the distance of third person. He doesn't say "they will eat and they will drink." He doesn't even say "it will be said to them."

No - He speaks directly: كُلُوا  - YOU eat! This forces us, the listeners and readers, to place ourselves in that scene. We're no longer observers thinking about "those people." We're being addressed. We're being invited to visualize ourselves there, being told directly by our Creator to eat and drink without worry or concern.

The word هَنِيئًا  means completely carefree - don't worry about checking labels for halal or haram. Everything there is pure and good.

But the most profound gift isn't even the food and drink. The greatest gift is that Allah ﷻ is speaking to us. Consider how much it means when someone important acknowledges your work. A father telling his son, "I saw your report card - I'm really proud of you." A boss acknowledging your project: "That was incredible work." Even a teacher's simple recognition can make your entire week.

Now imagine the Master of the heavens and earth, your Creator, directly addressing you: "Eat and drink, carefree, because of what you used to do." Allah ﷻ is acknowledging what you did. He's appreciating you, and you're appreciating Him. This divine recognition is itself among the greatest gifts of Paradise.

The ayah says بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ  - "because of what you used to do." Allah ﷻ attributes their reward to their actions, honoring them. Yet we know that even as Allah ﷻ says this, the believer's response will be: "No, this is from Your mercy alone. We don't deserve this." That's the beautiful exchange between Allah ﷻ and His grateful servants.

translation: They will be reclining on thrones lined up, and We will marry them to fair women with large, [beautiful] eyes
commentary:
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مَّصْفُوفَةٍ ۖ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ

"Reclining on couches arranged in rows, and We will wed them to companions with beautiful, striking eyes" - مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلَى سُرُرٍ مَصْفُوفَةٍ وَزَوَّجْنَاهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ. After the acknowledgment, after being told to eat and drink, now comes the gathering - the party.

The couches are already مَصْفُوفَة  - laid out in rows. When you host a large gathering, you have to rearrange all the furniture, move couches around, rent extra chairs. But this celebration is already prepared. There are many guests, so many seats are needed, all beautifully arranged.

Then comes the wedding: وَزَوَّجْنَاهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ - We will join them in marriage to حُور عِين  - companions whose eyes are captivating and beautiful. Notice the word used is زَوَّجْنَا  - "We married them." Just as in this world, we witnessed in the Quran that Allah ﷻ performed the marriage between Zaynab (RA) and the Prophet ﷺ, here too, Allah ﷻ Himself performs the marriage ceremony.

It's as if you must complete your education first - graduation comes first, then the celebration, then marriage.

translation: And those who believed and whose descendants followed them in faith - We will join with them their descendants, and We will not deprive them of anything of their deeds. Every person, for what he earned, is retained
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَـٰنٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَمَآ أَلَتْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۚ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ

"And those who believed, and whose descendants followed them in faith - We will join their descendants with them, and We will not decrease anything from their deeds. Every person is held in pledge for what they earned" - وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَانٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ.

Notice the beautiful precision: بِإِيمَانٍ - "with faith." Usually, we see the word bil-iman (with the definite article), but here it's simply "with faith," which can indicate that perhaps the later generations weren't at the same high level as their ancestors, but they still had genuine faith.

Imagine the scenario: some righteous believers reach the highest levels of Paradise - perhaps the seventh heaven. Their great-great-grandchildren are good people, but not quite at that super-elite level, so they're in a lower Paradise - perhaps the first or second heaven. The distance between these levels is like us looking at the stars - vast, different worlds entirely.

What happens? Should the higher ones come down, or the lower ones go up? Allah ﷻ says: أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ  - "We will join them together." The family members in the lower levels will be elevated and promoted because of their connection to their righteous ancestors. They get moved up so everyone can be together, because being reunited with family is one of the greatest joys.

But this raises a question: If they're being elevated beyond what their deeds alone earned, is that fair to those who worked harder? Allah ﷻ addresses this immediately: وَمَا أَلَتْنَاهُم مِّنْ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَيْءٍ - "And We will not decrease anything from their deeds."

The word أَلَتْنَا  means to give someone less than they deserve - like receiving a paycheck for $400 when you earned $500. Allah ﷻ assures us: the people at the higher levels won't have their rewards diminished because their family joined them. Everyone receives exactly what they earned, and the family reunion is an additional gift from Allah's generosity.

كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ - "Every person is held in pledge for what they earned." The word رَهِين  refers to collateral - like when you borrow money and leave your car title as security until you repay. Your deeds are your collateral. You present your deeds, and they become the means of your entry into Paradise. The exchange is just and precise, even as Allah's mercy makes it possible.

translation: And We will provide them with fruit and meat from whatever they desire
commentary:
وَأَمْدَدْنَـٰهُم بِفَـٰكِهَةٍ وَلَحْمٍ مِّمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ

"And We will provide them with fruit and meat from whatever they desire" - وَأَمْدَدْنَاهُم بِفَاكِهَةٍ وَلَحْمٍ مِّمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ. Notice that fruits are mentioned first, then meat. Some classical scholars suggested that perhaps there's wisdom here for our lives in this world too - maybe there's benefit in eating fruit before our meals, as it's presented first in the meal service of Paradise.

Muslim physicians, inspired by this ordering in the description of Paradise, conducted research and wrote fascinating articles about the health benefits of this sequence. Even in describing eternal rewards, the Quran inspires beneficial reflection for our temporary world.

The variety is emphasized: chicken shawarma is available, Adana kebab is there, lamb, goat, duck - every type of meat you could crave. You get to choose exactly what you want.

translation: They will exchange with one another a cup [of wine] wherein [results] no ill speech or commission of sin
commentary:
يَتَنَـٰزَعُونَ فِيهَا كَأْسًا لَّا لَغْوٌ فِيهَا وَلَا تَأْثِيمٌ

"They will playfully pass cups to one another - no idle talk in it, nor any cause of sin" - يَتَنَازَعُونَ فِيهَا كَأْسًا لَّا لَغْوٌ فِيهَا وَلَا تَأْثِيمٌ. The word يَتَنَازَعُونَ  means they're snatching cups from each other, playing around. Imagine the scene: people laughing, grabbing each other's drinks playfully, the kind of joyful interaction that happens when families and friends are completely comfortable together.

This isn't a formal, stuffy gathering where everyone sits in suits and ties, speaking carefully. You can let loose. You can knock someone's cup over, they knock yours over, you're wrestling and playing around. It's genuine, joyful fun - the kind that makes celebrations memorable.

لَا لَغْوٌ فِيهَا وَلَا تَأْثِيمٌ - "No idle talk in it, nor any cause of sin." Even though they're having tremendous fun, there won't be any empty, vulgar speech (لَغْو), and there won't be any تَأْثِيم  - anything that leads to sin or creates sin.

In this world, even innocent fun can sometimes lead to harm. Someone gets hurt, feelings get damaged, or play crosses a line into something harmful. But in Paradise, the fun is pure. The joy is complete and free from any negative consequence. This is celebration perfected - all the happiness, none of the regret.

translation: There will circulate among them [servant] boys [especially] for them, as if they were pearls well-protected
commentary:
۞ وَيَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ غِلْمَانٌ لَّهُمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لُؤْلُؤٌ مَّكْنُونٌ

Young servants will circulate among them, attending to their needs. The description كَأَنَّهُمْ لُؤْلُؤٌ مَّكْنُونٌ (as though they are hidden pearls) emphasizes their pristine appearance and dignity.

Why does Allah ﷻ mention the appearance of these servants? Consider when you enter an elite restaurant or catering establishment - the presentation of the servers reflects the quality of the entire experience. If servers arrive disheveled or unkempt, it affects your impression of the place. But when they're properly dressed and professional, it elevates the entire atmosphere.

These servants being described as "hidden pearls" - shiny, clean, protected - tells us about the level of honor and dignity in every aspect of Paradise. Even those serving are presented with the utmost care and respect. This attention to detail in their appearance adds to the magnificent aura of the whole experience.

translation: And they will approach one another, inquiring of each other
commentary:
وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ

The people of Paradise will turn to one another, asking each other questions. This is the beautiful social dimension of Paradise - believers reconnecting, reminiscing about their journey. "I remember you! How have you been? The life in the grave - it was long, wasn't it? Actually, it felt kind of short. And that Day of Judgment - that was intense, right? Remember when we crossed over the Sirat?"

These are casual conversations between old companions, friends who shared the struggle of faith in the worldly life. "Remember how we used to meet at the masjid? Remember how we'd hang out afterwards?" The bonds formed in worship and righteousness continue in Paradise, but now without any of the anxieties and difficulties of the worldly life.

translation: They will say, "Indeed, we were previously among our people fearful [of displeasing Allah]
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِىٓ أَهْلِنَا مُشْفِقِينَ

They will say, "Indeed, we used to be fearful before, among our families." The word مُشْفِقِينَ  carries the meaning of being deeply concerned, even worried. 

This ayah is particularly powerful because Allah ﷻ has already mentioned that families will be reunited in Paradise. Now these believers are reflecting: "I used to worry so much about my family. Which Islamic school should I enroll my children in? Where should we live to give them the best environment? What about when they go to college - will they be influenced negatively? Who will they marry? Will they find righteous spouses?"

All those fears parents carry about raising righteous children, all that stress about protecting their faith - it's being acknowledged here. And now, reunited with their families in Paradise, they realize: Alhamdulillah, it all worked out. Allah ﷻ took care of it all.

translation: So Allah conferred favor upon us and protected us from the punishment of the Scorching Fire
commentary:
فَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا وَوَقَىٰنَا عَذَابَ ٱلسَّمُومِ

"So Allah ﷻ conferred favor upon us and protected us from the punishment of the scorching fire." The word فَمَنَّ  indicates a tremendous favor, a blessing beyond what we deserved.

The term السَّمُوم  refers to a burning hot wind. If you've experienced desert climates - places like Arizona or the Arabian Peninsula - you know that when hot wind blows, it's not just uncomfortable. The wind itself burns. This is why traditional Arab clothing includes layers and turbans - they're not dying from the heat, they're actually protecting themselves from the scorching wind and sun.

The people of Paradise recognize that being saved from this punishment wasn't their own doing - it was pure favor from Allah ﷻ.

translation: Indeed, we used to supplicate Him before. Indeed, it is He who is the Beneficent, the Merciful
commentary:
إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلُ نَدْعُوهُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْبَرُّ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"Indeed, we used to call upon Him before. Indeed, He is the Most Kind, the Most Merciful."

The believers reflect on their relationship with Allah ﷻ in the worldly life. إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلُ نَدْعُوهُ - we were the ones who used to make dua to Him, who used to call on Him. And now they understand who He truly was all along: الْبَرُّ الرَّحِيمُ.

The name Al-Barr is particularly meaningful. While often translated as "the Source of All Goodness," the word بَرّ also means land. Why this connection? Because land is stable, solid. Earlier in this surah, we learned about هَوَى - walking on unstable, churning water where you don't know what lies beneath. Al-Barr is the opposite: He is the only source of stable goodness, the One who provides us with firm ground, with stability in our lives. He showed us constant love and endless mercy.

translation: So remind [O Muhammad], for you are not, by the favor of your Lord, a soothsayer or a madman
commentary:
فَذَكِّرْ فَمَآ أَنتَ بِنِعْمَتِ رَبِّكَ بِكَاهِنٍ وَلَا مَجْنُونٍ

After describing the reality of Paradise, Allah ﷻ now addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly: "So remind, for you are not, by the favor of your Lord, a soothsayer or a madman."

The instruction is simple: just keep reminding people. Some will hear all of this description of Paradise and be moved by it. They'll think about it, they'll want that eternal home. Others will dismiss it as a joke, and that's their choice. Your job is simply to remind.

The mention of كَاهِن (soothsayer) and مَجْنُون (madman) addresses the accusations being thrown at the Prophet ﷺ. You're not someone reading fortunes or making predictions based on supernatural claims, nor are you insane. You're delivering a message by the special favor of your Master.

translation: Or do they say [of you], "A poet for whom we await a misfortune of time
commentary:
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ شَاعِرٌ نَّتَرَبَّصُ بِهِۦ رَيْبَ ٱلْمَنُونِ

"Or do they say, 'He is a poet for whom we await the uncertainty of fate'?"

Now Allah ﷻ captures the dismissive attitude of the disbelievers. They're calling him a poet, but not taking any serious action. Their strategy? "Let's just wait and see what happens. Maybe this poetry phase will wear off. He's going through something - he'll snap out of it eventually."

The phrase رَيْبَ ٱلْمَنُونِ  refers to the doubt or uncertainty of time. The word مَنُون comes from the root meaning favor, because sometimes time brings favorable circumstances. They're hoping that time will work in their favor and the Prophet ﷺ will simply stop calling to this message.

This seems to indicate an early Makkan period when they hadn't yet decided how to deal with him. They're not even sure if this mission will last or if it's just a temporary phase.

translation: Say, "Wait, for indeed I am, with you, among the waiters
commentary:
قُلْ تَرَبَّصُوا۟ فَإِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُتَرَبِّصِينَ

"Say, 'Then wait, for indeed I am with you among those who wait.'"

The response is powerful in its simplicity. You want to wait and see? Fine. I'm waiting too. Let's see who's going through a phase.

This addresses a common experience for people of faith. When someone exhibits a significant transformation - especially a quick one - loved ones and those around them often dismiss it as temporary. "They'll snap out of it eventually." But the Prophet ﷺ demonstrates his commitment: No, you wait and see. I'm waiting along with you to see who's really committed and who's going through a phase.

translation: Or do their minds command them to [say] this, or are they a transgressing people
commentary:
أَمْ تَأْمُرُهُمْ أَحْلَـٰمُهُم بِهَـٰذَآ ۚ أَمْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ

"Or do their minds command them to this, or are they a transgressing people?"

The word أَحْلَـٰم  is one of the terms for intellect in Arabic. But أَحْلَـٰم specifically refers to considerate intellect - minds that seek to show mercy or courtesy to others.

Allah ﷻ is pointing out the irony: "Are their 'considerate' intellects telling them to be so courteous to you? Is that why they're saying, 'Oh, he's just a poet, let's be nice to him, we don't need to punish him right now'?" But while they're claiming to show consideration, they're actually in rebellion against Allah ﷻ. They're the ones who are طَاغُونَ - transgressors and violators. They need to be shown courtesy, not the Prophet ﷺ.

translation: Or do they say, "He has made it up"? Rather, they do not believe
commentary:
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ تَقَوَّلَهُۥ ۚ بَل لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ

"Or do they say, 'He has made it up'? Rather, they do not believe."

The word تَقَوَّلَ  means to force oneself to say something unnatural. It's when someone speaks in a way that doesn't fit their normal speech pattern, and people can tell something is off. If you suddenly started speaking in an overly formal, unnatural way, people would notice immediately.

The accusation was that this Quran doesn't sound like the Prophet ﷺ - he must be making it up, forcing himself to recite these words. But Allah ﷻ cuts through the excuse: بَل لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ - rather, the real issue is that they simply don't want to believe.

translation: Then let them produce a statement like it, if they should be truthful
commentary:
فَلْيَأْتُوا۟ بِحَدِيثٍ مِّثْلِهِۦٓ إِن كَانُوا۟ صَـٰدِقِينَ

"Then let them produce a statement like it, if they should be truthful."

The challenge is straightforward. If he made it up, then they can make it up too. If this is just human poetry or fabricated speech, then produce something similar. This is the Quran's recurring challenge to its critics - not just to criticize, but to produce something comparable.

translation: Or were they created by nothing, or were they the creators [of themselves]
commentary:
أَمْ خُلِقُوا۟ مِنْ غَيْرِ شَىْءٍ أَمْ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُونَ

"Or were they created by nothing, or were they the creators?"

Now the Quran addresses the fundamental question of existence and purpose. There are only logical possibilities here: either you were created out of nothing, or you created yourself. 

The atheist can construct elaborate arguments against the existence of God, but ultimately must take a massive leap of faith to claim that our intellect, our solar system, Earth's atmosphere, the intricate ecological balance that sustains life, the precise nutrients in soil, the genetic code - all of this was an accident. One giant accident traced back to an explosion.

The Big Bang theory essentially argues that you can put explosives in a container, blow it up, and it will transform into an ordered universe - specifically, a planet with the exact conditions necessary for complex life. It's suggesting that genetic code, with all its precision and complexity, came into being from chaos.

translation: Or did they create the heavens and the earth? Rather, they are not certain
commentary:
أَمْ خَلَقُوا۟ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۚ بَل لَّا يُوقِنُونَ

"Or did they create the heavens and earth? Rather, they are not certain."

If they didn't create themselves, did they create the vast universe around them? The answer is obvious. بَل لَّا يُوقِنُونَ - rather, they simply don't want to be certain. They're not seeking truth; they're avoiding conviction.

translation: Or have they the depositories [containing the provision] of your Lord? Or are they the controllers [of them]
commentary:
أَمْ عِندَهُمْ خَزَآئِنُ رَبِّكَ أَمْ هُمُ ٱلْمُصَۣيْطِرُونَ

"Or do they possess the depositories of your Lord, or are they the controllers?"

This addresses another angle of their rejection: "Why did this revelation come to him? If there is a God, fine - but why do we need a messenger? Why didn't He talk to us directly?"

The question exposes their arrogance. Do you own the treasures of your Lord - meaning, do you control when and how He sends His revelations? Are you ٱلْمُصَۣيْطِرُونَ - the ones in charge who get to make the calls?

This is the Quran's response to those who say, "If God really wanted to talk to me, He could talk to me directly. Why doesn't He?" The answer: because you don't get to make demands. He's not just God - He's your Master, and you're His slave. You don't get to set the conditions on what Allah ﷻ should or shouldn't do.

translation: Or have they a stairway [into the heaven] upon which they listen? Then let their listener produce a clear authority
commentary:
أَمْ لَهُمْ سُلَّمٌ يَسْتَمِعُونَ فِيهِ ۖ فَلْيَأْتِ مُسْتَمِعُهُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ

"Or do they have a stairway into the heaven upon which they listen? Then let their listener produce a clear authority."

Do they have some ladder to climb up to the heavens and eavesdrop on divine knowledge? If so, let the one they sent up there bring back some clear proof, some سُلْطَـٰن مُّبِين (clear authority) to support their worldview.

translation: Or has He daughters while you have sons
commentary:
أَمْ لَهُ ٱلْبَنَـٰتُ وَلَكُمُ ٱلْبَنُونَ

"Or does He have daughters while you have sons?"

This confronts the pagan Arab belief that angels were Allah's daughters - a belief they held while simultaneously considering daughters inferior and even burying them alive. The absurdity is laid bare: you claim God has daughters while you yourselves prefer sons?

translation: Or do you, [O Muhammad], ask of them a payment, so they are by debt burdened down
commentary:
أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ أَجْرًا فَهُم مِّن مَّغْرَمٍ مُّثْقَلُونَ

"Or do you ask them for payment, so they are burdened by debt?"

Now the questioning turns to the Prophet ﷺ himself. Are you asking them for compensation? Did you request anything in return for delivering this message?

The implication is that perhaps there was some backdoor negotiation: "Look, Muhammad, you have quite a few followers now and it's causing trouble. What do you really want? Money?" Did you name some price that's now weighing heavily on them as a burden?

Of course, the answer is no. The Prophet ﷺ never asked for anything.

translation: Or have they [knowledge of] the unseen, so they write [it] down
commentary:
أَمْ عِندَهُمُ ٱلْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ

"Or do they possess knowledge of the unseen, so they write it down?"

Do they have access to غَيْب (the unseen realm)? Are they recording divine knowledge? This is sarcasm aimed at their certainty in denying the message despite having no basis for their denial.

translation: Or do they intend a plan? But those who disbelieve - they are the object of a plan
commentary:
أَمْ يُرِيدُونَ كَيْدًا ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْمَكِيدُونَ

"Or do they intend a plan? But those who disbelieve - they are the object of the plan."

The verb used is يُرِيدُونَ  - present tense, indicating they're currently intending, still in the early phases of plotting something. They haven't executed any plan yet; they're just thinking about it.

But here's the reversal: those who disbelieve will find themselves the victims of their own scheming. The plot will turn back on them.

translation: Or have they a deity other than Allah? Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him
commentary:
أَمْ لَهُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

"Or do they have a deity other than Allah? Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him."

This is the culmination of all the questioning. Do they worship something other than Allah ﷻ? And regardless of their answer, سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ - Allah ﷻ is glorified and exalted far above whatever associations they make with Him.

translation: And if they were to see a fragment from the sky falling, they would say, "[It is merely] clouds heaped up
commentary:
وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كِسْفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ سَاقِطًا يَقُولُوا۟ سَحَابٌ مَّرْكُومٌ

"And if they were to see a fragment from the sky falling, they would say, 'It is merely clouds heaped up.'"

Even when punishment comes, they'll dismiss it. كِسْفًا  - a piece or fragment of the sky falling down - and their response? "Oh, it's just سَحَابٌ مَّرْكُومٌ - layers of clouds piled up."

This recalls the people of 'Ad, who saw clouds approaching their valley and began celebrating, thinking rain was coming. But those clouds brought destruction they couldn't have imagined.

translation: So leave them until they meet their Day in which they will be struck insensible
commentary:
فَذَرْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ يُصْعَقُونَ

"So leave them until they meet their Day in which they will be struck insensible."

فَذَرْهُمْ - leave them alone. Let them continue in their denial حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ - until they encounter their Day. The imagery is of lightning in those heavy clouds - they're waiting for rain, and instead lightning strikes them down, leaving them يُصْعَقُونَ - thunderstruck, devastated.

translation: The Day their plan will not avail them at all, nor will they be helped
commentary:
يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ

"The Day when their plan will not avail them at all, nor will they be helped."

On that Day, all their plotting, all their schemes and strategies - كَيْدُهُمْ won't benefit them in the slightest. And no one will be there to help them, to rescue them from what's coming.

translation: And indeed, for those who have wronged is a punishment before that, but most of them do not know
commentary:
وَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ عَذَابًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

"And indeed, for those who have wronged is a punishment before that, but most of them do not know."

The punishment described - that Day of striking - that's their ultimate punishment. But وَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ عَذَابًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ - those who wrong themselves have another punishment before that one, referring to worldly consequences and tribulations. Most of them don't recognize these signs or understand what's coming.

translation: And be patient, [O Muhammad], for the decision of your Lord, for indeed, you are in Our eyes. And exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord when you arise
commentary:
وَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا ۖ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ

"And be patient for the decision of your Lord, for indeed, you are in Our eyes. And glorify the praise of your Lord when you arise."

After all these confrontations with the disbelievers' arguments, Allah ﷻ addresses His Messenger ﷺ directly with comfort and guidance.

وَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ - remain patient with the wisdom, the judgment, the decree of your Lord. Why is patience emphasized? Because dealing with these people requires tremendous patience.

فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا - you are under Our eyes, under Our constant watch. We see everything you're going through. You're not alone in this struggle.

وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ - and glorify the praise of your Lord when you rise. This has beautiful layers of meaning. When the Prophet ﷺ has been sitting through a long session of delivering the message, and people mock him or walk away or refuse to listen, as he rises from that gathering, instead of expressing frustration or anger, he should say "SubhanAllah."

From this, we derive the Sunnah of ending gatherings with words of praise for Allah ﷻ - "Subhana Rabbika Rabbil 'izzati 'amma yasifun, wa salamun 'alal mursalin, wal hamdu lillahi Rabbil 'alamin."

But حِينَ تَقُومُ also means when you stand up in the middle of the night for prayer. So there are two kinds of standing: standing before people to deliver the message, and standing before Allah ﷻ in worship. In both situations, make tasbih.

translation: And in a part of the night exalt Him and after [the setting of] the stars
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَإِدْبَـٰرَ ٱلنُّجُومِ

"And in a part of the night exalt Him and after the setting of the stars."

The instruction continues: وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ - and during the night, glorify Him. This emphasizes the night prayer, the qiyam al-layl.

وَإِدْبَـٰرَ ٱلنُّجُومِ - and when the stars retreat, when they show their backs and disappear. This refers to the early morning time, just before Fajr, when the stars begin to fade. The Prophet ﷺ should already be awake before the stars disappear, and as they start to vanish, he continues making tasbih of Allah ﷻ.

The surah ends with this image of stars disappearing at dawn - a beautiful transition, because the next surah is Surat An-Najm (The Star). Just as At-Tur and Adh-Dhariyat share many parallel themes, An-Najm and Al-Qamar (The Moon) will have a complementary relationship, like stars and moon that appear together in the sky. This pairing continues through the Quran - Rahman and Al-Waqi'ah also share close connections. The surahs proceed in these meaningful pairs, each illuminating the other.
